title: Bangladesh Election Commission Announces Roadmap for Municipal Polls
summary: The EC has set a six-month timeline for holding elections across 12 city corporations, with voter list verification beginning next month.
content:
The Bangladesh Election Commission formally unveiled its roadmap on Monday for holding municipal elections across twelve city corporations, setting a six-month timeline that will see voter list verification begin as early as next month and polling conducted in phases through September 2026.

Chief Election Commissioner AMM Nasir Uddin announced the schedule at a press conference at the EC Secretariat in Agargaon, describing the process as the most technically prepared municipal election cycle in the commission's history. For the first time, all twelve corporations will use electronic voter verification machines at every polling station.

The announcement was broadly welcomed by major political parties, though the opposition pressed the commission to ensure a level playing field and guarantee the security of candidates and campaign workers in constituencies with a history of electoral violence.

International election observers from the European Union and the Carter Center have already confirmed their intention to deploy monitoring missions, a decision the EC described as a mark of international confidence in Bangladesh's democratic institutions.

Registration of candidacies is expected to open in mid-April, with the final voter lists published by the end of May. The EC stressed that no sitting official would be permitted to use state resources for campaign purposes, a guideline whose enforcement has been controversial in previous cycles.

title: Parliament Passes Landmark Digital Security Amendment After Heated Debate
summary: The amended law introduces new safeguards for journalists and limits the scope of provisions critics said were used to suppress press freedom.
content:
Bangladesh's National Parliament passed a significant amendment to the Digital Security Act late on Thursday after a seven-hour debate that saw unusually pointed exchanges between treasury and opposition benches, reflecting the depth of public concern about press freedom and civil liberties that has surrounded the legislation since its original passage.

The amended law introduces several key changes that civil society groups and journalist organizations had long demanded. Provisions that critics said were routinely weaponised against reporters, bloggers, and social media users have been narrowed, and new procedural safeguards require that any arrest under the act be preceded by a formal complaint reviewed by a designated magistrate rather than being initiated directly by law enforcement.

Law Minister Anisul Huq called the amendments a balanced response to legitimate concerns while preserving the state's capacity to prosecute genuine cybercrime and the spread of harmful misinformation. Opposition lawmakers, while welcoming some changes, argued the amendments did not go far enough and called for a complete repeal of provisions they described as incompatible with constitutional guarantees of free expression.

Editors Council of Bangladesh Secretary General expressed cautious optimism, saying the amended provisions represent meaningful progress but that the true test would be in their implementation and whether a culture change within law enforcement agencies would follow the legislative change.
